{{Short description|How location is linked to wealth}}
[[Image:GDP per capita (nominal) 2015.png|thumb|upright=1.4|[[Gross domestic product]] ([[Real versus nominal value|nominal]] per capita) in 2015

{{legend|#400000|over $64,000}} {{legend|#a00000|$32,000–64,000}} {{legend|#d00000|$16,000–32,000}} {{legend|#fd2a00|$8,000–16,000}} {{legend|#fe7733|$4,000–8,000}}{{legend|#fea933|$2,000–4,000}} {{legend|#fed24c|$1,000–2,000}} {{legend|#feff33|$500–1,000}} {{legend|#ffffa6|below $500}} {{legend|white|unavailable}}]]
'''Geography and wealth''' are a frequent subject of study in geography, both between nations and across regions within nations.<ref name=":0">[http://faculty.arec.umd.edu/jhorowitz/Income-Temp-i.pdf The Income-Temperature Relationship in a Cross-Section of Countries and its Implications for Global Warming]{{Dead link|date=May 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref> Some scholars, such as [[Jeffrey Sachs|Jeffrey D. Sachs]], argue that geography has a key role in the development of a nation's economic growth.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gallup |first1=John Luke |last2=Sachs |first2=Jeffrey D. |last3=Mellinger |first3=Andrew D. |date=August 1999 |title=Geography and Economic Development |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1177/016001799761012334 |journal=International Regional Science Review |volume=22 |issue=2 |pages=179–232 |doi=10.1177/016001799761012334 |s2cid=11559764 |issn=0160-0176|url-access=subscription }}</ref> For instance, nations that reside along coastal regions, or those who have access to a nearby water source, are more plentiful and able to trade with neighboring nations. In addition, countries with tropical climates face significant challenges, such as disease, extreme weather patterns, and lower agricultural productivity.

This thesis is supported by the fact that the volumes of UV radiation harm economic activity.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Andersen |first1=Thomas Barnebeck |last2=Dalgaard |first2=Carl-Johan |last3=Selaya |first3=Pablo |date=2016-02-23 |title=Climate and the Emergence of Global Income Differences |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1093/restud/rdw006 |journal=The Review of Economic Studies |volume=83 |issue=4 |pages=1334–1363 |doi=10.1093/restud/rdw006 |issn=0034-6527|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Several studies confirm that spatial development in countries with higher levels of economic development differs from countries with lower levels of development.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Henderson |first1=J Vernon |last2=Squires |first2=Tim |last3=Storeygard |first3=Adam |last4=Weil |first4=David |date=2017-09-11 |title=The Global Distribution of Economic Activity: Nature, History, and the Role of Trade1 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjx030 |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |volume=133 |issue=1 |pages=357–406 |doi=10.1093/qje/qjx030 |pmid=31798191 |pmc=6889963 |issn=0033-5533}}</ref> The correlation between geography and a nation's wealth can be observed by examining a country's GDP (gross national product) per capita, which takes into account a nation's economic output and population.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Sachs|first=Jeffrey D.|date=2001|title=The Geography of Poverty and Wealth|journal=Scientific American|volume=284|issue=3|pages=70–5|doi=10.1038/scientificamerican0301-70|pmid=11234509|bibcode=2001SciAm.284c..70S}}</ref>

The wealthiest nations of the world with the highest [[standard of living]] tend to be those at the northern extreme of areas open to human habitation—including Northern Europe, the United States, and Canada. Within prosperous nations, wealth often increases with distance from the equator. Researchers at [[Harvard]]'s Center for International Development found in 2001 that only two tropical economies — [[Singapore]] and [[Hong Kong]] — are classified as high- income by the [[World Bank]], while all countries within regions zoned as [[temperate]] had either middle- or high-income economies.<ref>{{Cite journal|journal=NBER Working Paper Series |last=Sachs |first=Jeffrey |date=February 2001 |title=Tropical Underdevelopment |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.3386/w8119 |location=Cambridge, MA|doi=10.3386/w8119 |s2cid=346806 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Measurement==

Most of the recent studies use national [[gross domestic product]] per person, as measured by the [[World Bank]] and the [[International Monetary Fund]], as the unit of comparison. Intra-national comparisons use their own data, and political divisions, such as states or provinces, delineate the study areas.

==Distance from the Equator==

In prosperous nations, a greater distance from the equator correlates with higher wealth. For example, the [[Northeast United States]] has long been wealthier than its southern counterpart, and [[northern Italy]] wealthier than [[Southern Italy|southern regions]] of the country. Even within Africa, this effect is evident, as the nations farthest from the equator are wealthier. In Africa, the wealthiest nations are the three on the southern tip of the continent, [[South Africa]], [[Botswana]], and [[Namibia]], and the countries of [[North Africa]]. Similarly, in South America, [[Argentina]], [[Southern Brazil]], [[Chile]], and [[Uruguay]] have long been the wealthiest. Within [[Asia]], [[Indonesia]], located on the equator, is among the poorest. Within Central Asia, Kazakhstan is wealthier than other former Soviet Republics which border it to the south, like Uzbekistan.<ref>[http://www.imf.org/external/pubs/ft/weo/2006/02/data/weorept.aspx?sy=2005&ey=2005&scsm=1&ssd=1&sort=country&ds=.&br=1&c=512%2C446%2C914%2C666%2C612%2C668%2C614%2C672%2C311%2C946%2C213%2C137%2C911%2C962%2C193%2C674%2C122%2C676%2C912%2C548%2C313%2C556%2C419%2C678%2C513%2C181%2C316%2C682%2C913%2C684%2C124%2C273%2C339%2C921%2C638%2C948%2C514%2C686%2C218%2C688%2C963%2C518%2C616%2C728%2C223%2C558%2C516%2C138%2C918%2C353%2C748%2C196%2C618%2C278%2C522%2C692%2C622%2C694%2C156%2C142%2C624%2C449%2C626%2C564%2C628%2C283%2C228%2C853%2C924%2C288%2C233%2C293%2C632%2C566%2C636%2C964%2C634%2C182%2C238%2C453%2C662%2C968%2C960%2C922%2C423%2C714%2C935%2C862%2C128%2C716%2C611%2C456%2C321%2C722%2C243%2C965%2C248%2C718%2C469%2C724%2C253%2C576%2C642%2C936%2C643%2C961%2C939%2C813%2C644%2C199%2C819%2C184%2C172%2C524%2C132%2C361%2C646%2C362%2C648%2C364%2C915%2C732%2C134%2C366%2C652%2C734%2C174%2C144%2C328%2C146%2C258%2C463%2C656%2C528%2C654%2C923%2C336%2C738%2C263%2C578%2C268%2C537%2C532%2C742%2C944%2C866%2C176%2C369%2C534%2C744%2C536%2C186%2C429%2C925%2C178%2C746%2C436%2C926%2C136%2C466%2C343%2C112%2C158%2C111%2C439%2C298%2C916%2C927%2C664%2C846%2C826%2C299%2C542%2C582%2C443%2C474%2C917%2C754%2C544%2C698%2C941&s=PPPPC&grp=0&a=&pr1.x=56&pr1.y=10 Gross domestic product based on purchasing-power-parity (PPP) per capita GDP in 2005]</ref> Very often such differences in economic development are linked to the [[Global North and Global South|North-South]] issue.<ref>{{Cite web |title=North-South Problem |url=https://www.mofa.go.jp/policy/other/bluebook/1971/1971-1-15.htm |access-date=2023-05-27 |website=www.mofa.go.jp}}</ref> This approach assumes an empirical division of the world into rich northern countries and poor southern countries.

In addition, the problem of heterogeneous economic development (between the industrialised north and the agrarian south) also exists within the following countries:<ref>{{Cite journal|journal=NBER Working Paper Series |last1=Alesina |first1=Alberto |last2=Devleeschauwer |first2=Arnaud |last3=Easterly |first3=William |last4=Kurlat |first4=Sergio |last5=Wacziarg |first5=Romain |date=January 2003 |title=Fractionalization |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.3386/w9411 |location=Cambridge, MA|doi=10.3386/w9411 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

* [[Turkistan Region|South]] and [[North Kazakhstan Region|North]] Kazakhstan.
* Southern ([[Osh]], [[Batken]]) and northern ([[Bishkek]] city and Chui) oblasts in [[Kyrgyzstan]].
* [[Southern Italy]] and [[Padania]].
* [[Flanders]] and [[Wallonia]] in [[Belgium]].
* [[Catalonia]], [[Basque Country (autonomous community)|Basque Country]] and the rest of [[Spain]].
* [[New England]] and the [[Southeastern United States|south-eastern states]] of the USA.
* [[Poland A and B]].
* North (Mediterranean Coast) and South (Vast Saharan Region) in [[Algeria]].

==Explanations==

===Historic interpretations===
One of the first to describe and assess the phenomenon was the [[France|French]] [[philosopher]] [[Charles de Secondat, Baron de Montesquieu|Montesquieu]], who asserted in the 18th century that "cold air constringes (''sic'') the extremities of the external fibres of the body; this increases their elasticity, and favours the return of the blood from the extreme parts to the heart. It contracts those very fibres; consequently, it also increases their force. On the contrary, warm air relaxes and lengthens the extremes of the fibres; of course, it diminishes their force and elasticity. People are therefore more vigorous in cold climates."<ref>{{Cite journal |last=KRIESEL |first=KARL MARCUS |title=Montesquieu: Possibilistic Political Geographer |date=September 1968 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8306.1968.tb01652.x |journal=Annals of the Association of American Geographers |volume=58 |issue=3 |pages=557–574 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8306.1968.tb01652.x |issn=0004-5608|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The 19th century historian [[Henry Thomas Buckle]] wrote that "climate, soil, food, and the aspects of nature are the primary causes of intellectual progress—the first three indirectly, through determining the accumulation and [[distribution of wealth]], and the last by directly influencing the accumulation and distribution of thought, the imagination being stimulated and the understanding subdued when the phenomena of the external world are sublime and terrible, the understanding being emboldened and the imagination curbed when they are small and feeble."

The [[Industrial Revolution|first industrial revolution]] marked the beginning of divergence among nations. Alex Trew introduces a spatial take-off model that uses data on occupations in 18th-century [[England]]. The model predicts changes in the [[spatial distribution]] of agricultural and manufacturing employment, which are consistent with the 1817 and 1861 data.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Trew |first=Alex |date=October 2014 |title=Spatial takeoff in the first industrial revolution |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.red.2014.01.002 |journal=Review of Economic Dynamics |volume=17 |issue=4 |pages=707–725 |doi=10.1016/j.red.2014.01.002 |issn=1094-2025|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Thus, one of the historical factors influencing the geographical unevenness of wealth distribution is the degree of industrial development, catalysed by the Industrial Revolution.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oddy |first=Nicholas |date=2016 |title=Industrial Revolution |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781472596161-bed-online-042 |journal=The Bloomsbury Encyclopedia of Design |doi=10.5040/9781472596161-bed-online-042|isbn=9781472596161 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

===Climatic differences ===

Physiologist [[Jared Diamond]] was inspired to write his [[Pulitzer Prize]]-winning work ''[[Guns, Germs, and Steel]]'' by a question posed by [[Yali (politician)|Yali]], a [[New Guinea]]n politician: why were Europeans so much wealthier than his people? <ref>{{Cite journal |last=King |first=Arthur B. |date=July 2001 |title=Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies by Jared Diamond |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.3138/guthrie.70.3.119 |journal=The Guthrie Journal |volume=70 |issue=3 |pages=119 |doi=10.3138/guthrie.70.3.119 |s2cid=258662777 |issn=0882-696X}}</ref> In this book, Diamond argues that the [[Europe]]-[[Asia]] ([[Eurasia]]) land mass is particularly favorable for the transition of societies from [[hunter-gatherer]] to farming communities. This continent stretches much farther along the same lines of latitude than any other continent. Since it is much easier to transfer a domesticated species along the same latitude than it is to move it to a warmer or colder climate, any species developed at a particular latitude will be transferred across the continent in a relatively short amount of time. Thus, the inhabitants of the Eurasian continent have had a built-in advantage in terms of earlier development of farming, and a greater range of plants and animals from which to choose. [[Oded Galor]], and Ömer Özak examine existing differences in time preference across countries and regions, using pre-industrial agro-climatic characteristics.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Galor |first1=Oded |last2=Özak |first2=Ömer |date=2016-10-01 |title=The Agricultural Origins of Time Preference |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1257/aer.20150020 |journal=American Economic Review |volume=106 |issue=10 |pages=3064–3103 |doi=10.1257/aer.20150020 |pmid=28781375 |pmc=5541952 |issn=0002-8282}}</ref> The authors conclude that agro-climatic characteristics have a significant impact through culture on economic behaviour, the degree of technology adoption and human capital.

==== Impact of global warming ====
In a 2006 paper discussing the potential impact of [[global warming]] on wealth, John K. Horowitz of the [[University of Maryland, College Park|University of Maryland]] predicted that a 2-degree Fahrenheit ({{convert|2|F-change|C-change|disp=out|0}}) temperature increase across all countries would cause a decrease of 2 to 6 percent in world GDP, with a best estimate of around 3.5 percent.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Income-Temperature Relationship in a Cross-Section of Countries and its Implications for Global Warming |url=http://www.arec.umd.edu/people/faculty/jhorowitz/Paper4_4.pdf |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060908203840/http://www.arec.umd.edu/people/faculty/jhorowitz/Paper4_4.pdf |archive-date=2006-09-08 |access-date=2007-03-15}}</ref> The former [[United Nations]] Secretary-General [[Ban Ki-moon]] had expressed concern that global warming would exacerbate the existing poverty in Africa.<ref>[https://www.un.org/apps/news/story.asp?NewsID=21448&Cr=climate&Cr1 Poor countries will suffer most from global warming, Ban Ki-moon warns]</ref> There is growing evidence that the inequality between rich and poor people's emissions within countries now exceeds the inequality between countries. High-emitting countries have more in common across international borders, regardless of where they live. It is noted that personal wealth explains more than national wealth the sources of emissions.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Roston |first1=Eric |last2=Kaufman |first2=Leslie |last3=Warren |first3=Hayley |date=2022-03-24 |title=How the World's Richest People Are Driving Global Warming |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/graphics/2022-wealth-carbon-emissions-inequality-powers-world-climate/ |access-date=2023-05-27 |work=Bloomberg.com |language=en}}</ref>

=== Cultural differences ===
One important determinant of unequal wealth in spatial contexts is significant cultural differences, including issues of attitudes to women and the slave trade. Existing research finds that the descendants of societies that traditionally practised [[plough]] farming now have less equal gender norms. The evidence is strong and persistent across countries, areas within countries, and ethnic groups within areas.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Alesina |first1=Alberto |last2=Giuliano |first2=Paola |last3=Nunn |first3=Nathan |date=2013-05-01 |title=On the Origins of Gender Roles: Women and the Plough |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjt005 |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |volume=128 |issue=2 |pages=469–530 |doi=10.1093/qje/qjt005 |issn=0033-5533}}</ref> This research is particularly relevant in terms of the division of labour and equality as a basic mechanism that contributes to economic development. Part of the work is devoted to analyzing the slave trade as a cultural element of spatial development. [[Nathan Nunn]], and [[Leonard Wantchekon]] explore the differences in levels of donorisation in [[Africa]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nunn |first1=Nathan |last2=Wantchekon |first2=Leonard |date=2011-12-01 |title=The Slave Trade and the Origins of Mistrust in Africa |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1257/aer.101.7.3221 |journal=American Economic Review |volume=101 |issue=7 |pages=3221–3252 |doi=10.1257/aer.101.7.3221 |s2cid=219336147 |issn=0002-8282}}</ref> The authors find that people whose ancestors were heavily raided during the slave trade are less trusting today. The authors used several strategies to determine trust and found that the link is causal. Furthermore, it is noted that the rugged terrain in Africa protected those who were raided during the slave trade. Given that the [[Slavery|slave]] trade inhibited subsequent economic development, the rugged terrain in Africa has also had a historically indirect positive effect on income.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Nunn |first1=Nathan |last2=Puga |first2=Diego |date=February 2012 |title=Ruggedness: The Blessing of Bad Geography in Africa |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1162/rest_a_00161 |journal=Review of Economics and Statistics |volume=94 |issue=1 |pages=20–36 |doi=10.1162/rest_a_00161 |s2cid=16512034 |issn=0034-6535}}</ref> Another factor determining spatial development is the distance to pre-industrial technological frontiers. It has been noted that it can contribute to the emergence of a culture conducive to innovation, knowledge creation and entrepreneurship.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Özak |first=Ömer |date=2018-03-14 |title=Distance to the pre-industrial technological frontier and economic development |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s10887-018-9154-6 |journal=Journal of Economic Growth |volume=23 |issue=2 |pages=175–221 |doi=10.1007/s10887-018-9154-6 |s2cid=254656955 |issn=1381-4338|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Unequal technology development ===
Diamond notes that modern technologies and institutions were designed primarily in a small area of northwestern Europe, which is also supported by [[Oded Galor|Galor]]'s book "The journey of humanity: the origins of wealth and inequality".<ref>{{Cite book |last=Galor |first=Oded |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=oWQ0EAAAQBAJ |title=The Journey of Humanity: The Origins of Wealth and Inequality |date=2022-03-22 |publisher=Penguin |isbn=978-0-593-18601-5 |language=en}}</ref> After the [[Scientific Revolution]] in Europe in the 16th century, the quality of life increased and wealth began to spread to the middle class. This included agricultural techniques, machines, and medicines. These technologies and models readily spread to areas colonized by Europe, which happened to have similar climates, such as North America and Australia. As these areas also became centres of innovation, this bias was further enhanced. Technologies ranging from automobiles to power lines are more often designed for colder, drier regions, since most of their customers are from these regions.

The book ''[[Guns, Germs, and Steel]]'' goes on to document a [[feedback]] effect whereby technologies designed for the wealthy make them wealthier and thus more able to fund technological development. He notes that the far north has not always been the wealthiest latitude; until only a few centuries ago, the wealthiest belt stretched from Southern Europe through the [[Middle East]], northern [[India]] and southern [[China]], which was also highlighted in the book of [[Joel Mokyr|Mokyr]] "The lever of riches: Technological creativity and economic progress".<ref>{{Cite journal |date=October 1991 |title=Joel Mokyr. ''The Lever of Riches: Technological Creativity and Economic Progress''. New York: Oxford University Press. 1990. Pp. ix, 349. $24.95 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1086/ahr/96.4.1164 |journal=The American Historical Review |doi=10.1086/ahr/96.4.1164 |issn=1937-5239|url-access=subscription }}</ref> A dramatic shift in technologies, beginning with ocean-going ships and culminating in the [[Industrial Revolution]], saw the most developed belt move north into northern Europe, China, and the Americas. Northern Russia became a [[superpower]], while southern [[India]] became impoverished and colonized. Diamond argues that such dramatic changes demonstrate that the current distribution of wealth is not only due to immutable factors such as climate or [[Race (classification of human beings)|race]], citing the early emergence of [[agriculture]] in ancient [[Mesopotamia]] as evidence.

Diamond also notes the feedback effect on the growth of medical technology, stating that more research funding goes toward curing the ailments of Northerners.

===Disease===

[[Tick]]s, [[mosquito]]s, and [[rat]]s, which continue to be important disease [[Vector (epidemiology)|vectors]], benefit from warmer temperatures and higher humidities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Prillaman |first=McKenzie |date=2022-08-12 |title=Climate change is making hundreds of diseases much worse |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-022-02167-z |journal=Nature |language=en |doi=10.1038/d41586-022-02167-z|pmid=35962039 |s2cid=251540065 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> There has long been a [[malaria|malarial belt]] spanning the equatorial portions of the [[globe]]; the disease is especially deadly to children under the age of five.<ref name=":5">[https://www.cdc.gov/malaria/impact/statistics.htm Centers for Disease Control: Statistics and Malaria's Public Health Impact]</ref> Notably, it has been almost impossible for most forms of northern [[livestock]] to thrive due to the [[Endemic (epidemiology)|endemic]] presence of the [[tsetse fly]].<ref name=":4">{{Cite web | first = I. E. | last = Muriithi | url=http://www.ilri.org/InfoServ/Webpub/Fulldocs/Inagura/Impact.htm |title=The Impact and Implementation of Theileriosis and Trypanosomyasis on Livestock Production in Africa |access-date=2007-03-14 |archive-date=2007-09-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927190341/http://www.ilri.org/InfoServ/Webpub/Fulldocs/Inagura/Impact.htm |url-status=dead }}</ref> Bleakley finds empirical evidence that ankylostomosis eradication in the American South contributed to a significant increase in income that coincided with eradication activities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bleakley |first=H. |date=2007-02-01 |title=Disease and Development: Evidence from Hookworm Eradication in the American South |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1162/qjec.121.1.73 |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |volume=122 |issue=1 |pages=73–117 |doi=10.1162/qjec.121.1.73 |pmid=24146438 |pmc=3800113 |issn=0033-5533}}</ref>

Jared Diamond has linked the domestication of animals in Europe and Asia to the emergence of diseases that enabled these regions to conquer inhabitants of other continents. The close association of people in Eurasia with their domesticated animals provided a vector for the rapid transmission of diseases. Inhabitants of lands with few domesticated species were never exposed to the same range of diseases. So, at least on the American continents, they succumbed to diseases introduced from Eurasia. These effects were exhaustively discussed in [[William Hardy McNeill|William McNeill's]] book ''Plagues and Peoples''.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://worldhistoryconnected.press.uiuc.edu/3.1/br_whelchel.html |title=World History Connected: Review of ''Plagues and Peoples'' |access-date=2007-03-15 |archive-date=2008-10-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081006204242/http://worldhistoryconnected.press.uiuc.edu/3.1/br_whelchel.html |url-status=dead }}</ref> Besides, Ola Olsson, and Douglas A. Hibbs argue that geographical and initial biogeographical conditions had a decisive influence on the location and timing of the transition to sedentary agriculture, complex social organisation and, ultimately, to modern industrial production.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Olsson |first1=Ola |last2=Hibbs |first2=Douglas A. |date=May 2005 |title=Biogeography and long-run economic development |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.euroecorev.2003.08.010 |journal=European Economic Review |volume=49 |issue=4 |pages=909–938 |doi=10.1016/j.euroecorev.2003.08.010 |s2cid=15773734 |issn=0014-2921|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The 2001 Harvard study mentions high [[infant mortality]] as another factor; since birth rates usually increase in compensation, women may delay their entry into the workforce to care for their younger children. The education of the surviving children then becomes difficult, perpetuating a cycle of poverty. Bloom, Canning, and Fink argue that health in childhood affects productivity in adulthood.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Bloom |first1=David |last2=Canning |first2=David |last3=Fink |first3=Günther |date=July 2009 |title=Disease and Development Revisited | work = NBER Working Paper Series | publisher = National Bureau of Economic Research | url=http://dx.doi.org/10.3386/w15137 |location=Cambridge, MA|doi=10.3386/w15137 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

=== Landlockedness ===
Countries without direct access to the sea—commonly referred to as [[Landlocked country|landlocked countries]]—face persistent structural challenges that hinder their [[economic development]]. Research shows that [[landlocked developing countries]] (LLDCs) typically experience slower economic growth, export less, and have lower [[per capita income]] compared to coastal nations. This is due to higher transportation costs, dependence on neighboring transit countries, and exposure to political instability and administrative inefficiencies across borders.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Faye |first1=Michael L. | first2 = John W. | last2 = McArthur | first3 = Jeffrey D. | last3 = Sachs |last4=Snow |first4=Thomas |date=2004-03-01 |title=The Challenges Facing Landlocked Developing Countries |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/14649880310001660201 |journal=Journal of Human Development |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=31–68 |doi=10.1080/14649880310001660201 |issn=1464-9888|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Additionally, landlocked countries are frequently cut off from economic sectors such as [[international tourism]], [[Maritime transport|maritime trade]], and coastal resource industries. These disadvantages contribute to a substantial growth penalty: a cross-country analysis found that landlocked countries grew on average 1.5 percentage points more slowly per year than similar coastal countries, even after accounting for differences in investment levels and income.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Mackellar |first=Landis |last2=Wörgötter |first2=Andreas |last3=Wörz |first3=Julia |date=2000-01-01 |title=Economic Development Problems of Landlocked Countries |url=https://irihs.ihs.ac.at/id/eprint/1230/ |access-date=2025-04-16 |website=irihs.ihs.ac.at |language=en}}</ref>

===Other===

*In "Climate and Scale in Economic Growth", William A. Masters and Margaret S. McMillan of [[Purdue University]] and [[Tufts University]] hypothesize that the disparity is partially due to the effects of frost in increasing soil [[Fertility (soil)|fertility]].<ref>[http://www.ksg.harvard.edu/CID/cidwp/048.pdf Climate and Scale in Economic Growth]</ref>
*Hernando Zuleta, of the [[Universidad del Rosario]], has proposed that where output fluctuations are more profound, i.e., areas that experience winter, saving is more pronounced, which leads to the adoption or creation of capital-intensive technologies.<ref>[https://ssrn.com/abstract=901794 Seasons, Savings, and Gdp]</ref>
*Daron Acemoglu, Simon Johnson, and James A. Robinson of [[MIT]] argued in 2001 that in places where Europeans faced high mortality rates, they could not settle and were more likely to set up exploitative institutions. These institutions offered no protection for private property or checks and balances against government expropriation.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://econ-www.mit.edu/faculty/download_pdf.php?id=144 |title=The Colonial Origins of Comparative Development: An Empirical Investigation |access-date=2007-03-15 |archive-date=2007-03-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070302175707/http://econ-www.mit.edu/faculty/download_pdf.php?id=144 |url-status=dead }}</ref> They assert that after controlling for the effect of institutions, countries in Africa or those closer to the equator do not have lower incomes. This work has been disputed by [[David Albouy]], who argues that European mortality rates in the study were badly mismeasured, falsely supporting its conclusion.<ref>[http://repositories.cdlib.org/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1055&context=iber/cider The Colonial Origins of Comparative Development: An Investigation of the Settler Mortality Data]</ref>
*The authors of "Brain size, cranial morphology, climate, and time machines" assert that colder climates increase brain size, resulting in an intelligence differential.<ref>[https://www.jstor.org/stable/2742800 Brain size, cranial morphology, climate, and time machines (and Comments and Reply)]</ref>
*Dao, N.T., and Davila, J. argue that when several geographical factors coincide, the economy may become locked in Malthusian stagnation and never develop.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dao |first1=Nguyen Thang |last2=Dávila |first2=Julio |date=September 2013 |title=Can geography lock a society in stagnation? |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.econlet.2013.05.031 |journal=Economics Letters |volume=120 |issue=3 |pages=442–446 |doi=10.1016/j.econlet.2013.05.031 |issn=0165-1765|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Further reading==
*Jared Diamond: ''Guns, Germs, and Steel: The Fates of Human Societies''. W.W. Norton & Company, March 1997. {{ISBN|0-393-03891-2}}
*Iain Hay: ''Geographies of the Super-rich''. Edward Elgar. 2013 {{ISBN|978-0-85793-568-7}}
*Iain Hay and Jonathan Beaverstock: ''Handbook on Wealth and the Super-rich''. Edward Elgar {{ISBN|978-1-78347-403-5}} 
*Theil, Henri, and Dongling Chen, "The Equatorial Grand Canyon," De Economist (1995). Abstract at [https://archive.today/20130203023812/http://www.springerlink.com/content/n3k1162q725h51j0/]
*[http://www.geo.utep.edu/pub/nick_miller/1310/LECTURE_21.html University of Texas at El Paso. Economic Geography: Lecture 21]
*Sachs, Jeffrey D., Andrew D. Mellinger, and John L. Gallup. 2001. The Geography of Poverty and Wealth. Scientific American. March 2001.
*McNeill, William H. ''Plagues and People''. New York: Anchor Books, 1976. {{ISBN|0-385-12122-9}}. Reprinted with a new preface in 1998.
